Justification: The quarterly account health review was selected because it combines the highest weighted score (4.35) with the strongest balance across all five dimensions. The 0.55-point gap over the runner-up exceeds the methodology's 0.5-point tiebreaker threshold, so the Feasibility tiebreaker rule was not needed to resolve the selection — though notably, Cross-team Standup Prep would have won on Feasibility (5 vs 4) had a tiebreaker been required. Cross-team Standup Prep (3.80) scored highest on Feasibility (5), Complexity (5), and Risk Tolerance (5) — it is the simplest and safest workflow to build — but its Impact score of 2 reflects only ~2 hours/month of near-mechanical data gathering, severely limiting the value delivered by automation. Renewal Preparation (3.55) offered stronger Impact (4) but was weakened by Feasibility (3) due to unconfirmed finance system API access and Risk Tolerance (3) given the commercial stakes of renewal negotiations. The health review hits the optimal balance: high time savings (6-8 hrs per account per quarter across multiple accounts), strong data access (four API-accessible sources), a safe failure mode (internal review before any client exposure), and a gather-analyse-synthesise-report pattern that directly transfers to 3-4 other workflows — making it the highest-leverage first build for learning the framework.
Known Risks or Caveats: Product usage data from Mixpanel may require custom extraction depending on platform configuration and available export endpoints. Qualitative signals from Slack conversations are harder to capture and interpret programmatically — the agent may need to surface relevant threads for human review rather than attempting to synthesise sentiment autonomously. The synthesis step (turning raw data into health dimension scores) involves LLM judgement that will likely require iterative prompt refinement to reach acceptable accuracy.
Disqualified Candidates: Exec Business Review Prep (composite: 3.25) — Disqualifier 1: The workflow's value comes from the relationship built during the process, not the output. EBR preparation forces deep engagement with the account relationship and exec sponsor priorities. The prep process IS the relationship-building — automating it removes the thing that makes it valuable. Despite scoring Impact 5 (highest on the inventory alongside the chosen workflow), the disqualifier removes it from ranking entirely.
Dependencies from Stage 1: Stage 1 inventory confirmed all four automation signals as Strong for the chosen workflow: (1) Structural Repeatability — identical sequence for every account (pull CRM data, pull tickets, pull usage, pull Slack threads, score each dimension, write narrative, compile report); (2) Determinism — output follows directly from data + scoring framework with minimal subjective judgement; (3) Data Availability — Salesforce API, Zendesk API, Mixpanel export API, and Slack API all confirmed available; (4) Error Recoverability — report goes through internal review before reaching the client. Stage 1 also identified that this workflow shares data sources (Salesforce, Zendesk, Mixpanel) with Renewal Preparation and EBR Prep — the data-retrieval integrations built for Health Review create reusable infrastructure for the next automation target. The Stage 1 assessment confirmed no sub-workflow split was needed — all steps are automatable with a single human-in-the-loop review checkpoint before delivery.
